speaker: interviewer
text: --Tuesday the 30th of January, 1996. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing the Reverend Dr. Isaac Levy. We're in London, England. And this is tape 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yossele Rosensaft, as I said, was the-- although he might have called himself self-appointed leader of, of the free element, he was genuinely recognized as the person who-- as the spokesman. Interestingly enough, wherever he, he wished to put a case across, he would insist on speaking Yiddish. He had an interpreter. But he said, this is my language. And this is how I'm going to speak, almost like Gorbachev, in, in Russia. Anyhow, he spoke. Earl Harrison came in. And he addressed him. And he held forth to Earl Harrison. It was interpreted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had a word to say. And I can remember exactly what I said to him. Because I was so incensed that he came over here in the name of the president of United States. What is he going to see? What is he going to convey?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember saying to him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what does it say on the base of the 'Give me your'-- you know the-- you're welcoming the, the, the poor and the scattered remnants of, of humanity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you're leaving these people to rot in the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unless you change the wording on the base-- or if you wish to remain true to that, then you will make sure that these people are given the opportunity of leaving this place to the country in which they wish to go. He noted it and said goodbye-- never heard another word. He'd done his duty. He'd probably go back to the United States. He'll report he'd been to Belsen, he'd been to Buchenwald, he'd been to Dachau. And he will have a, a very nice report to, to render. And that was it. And it didn't leave me very, very satisfied.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, there are one or two little items of interest. You know what? I keep on saying you felt frustrated by the attitude of the authorities. You know, in hindsight, one wonders, were they prejudiced? Were they biased? Or were they just ignorant? Or was there, amongst some of them, a very-- a streak of unpleasantness?

speaker: interviewee
text: One incident I shall never forget, the senior staff chaplain who was one pip higher than me-- he was a lieutenant colonel. I was only a major. He heard I was going back to London.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, whilst you are there, do me a favor. I need a new cassock. Could you make inquiries for me? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew that there was two clerical tailors in London. And whilst I was there, I phoned them up. And I learned the situation. And I came back. I reported. I said, you know, clothes are still on rations. You need coupons. And there are two qualities of cloth for cassock. One is X number of coupons. One is Y number of coupons. Instead of saying to me, well, thank you very much for the trouble, he said to me, of course, I should've known better. Your people have cornered the cloth market. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, he was my superior in rank. But I forgot it. When I left, he was in a high dudgeon and said to him, you are an ungrateful bastard. And I walked out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was the one and only occasion in the whole of this sad chapter I had ever come across somebody who would have a nasty thing to say as-- on a-- sort of an anti-Semitic tone, coming from a chaplain in the army, which was a mistake. Because it was so contrary to-- I had not come across it the whole of my career, whether it was in the Middle East, or whether it was here, in Germany for example. Before we entered across the Rhine, I had taken a Seder service in a place called Goch, between the Rhine and the Meuse. And the brigadier who was in charge of supplies was somebody whom I had known because I-- whilst I was in England, I was stationed-- my wife and I were stationed with his sister. So we knew each other quite well. And, of course, when he knew who I was, everything I wanted. He, he seconded his army catering officer to, to be of service to me. So we had a Seder service. Now, that was a sort of thing that existed. There was a-- the padre was treated with tremendous respect. And yet here was one chaplain to another that behaved so disgustingly. And yet when you get a man like Brigadier Glyn Hughes, whose behavior towards us was such that he felt a, a sense of brotherhood with these people. And that here was a man who was stationed inside Germany and yet behaves like this. Well, that's the sort of attitude that goes on. There's one other case-- I must mention this. VE Day arrived. And there was an occasion for us to celebrate. So I arranged that I should have a service in Hamburg, of all places, which was the nearest big town to, to, to, to Belsen. Well, I needed accommodation, a hall, a place to meet. Hamburg had been badly blitzed. So I called on the office of the Burgermeister, so the mayor of Hamburg. He wasn't there. But his deputy was.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my best German, I asked him, what accommodation there was available? He wasn't very helpful. I said, by the way, where are the synagogues in-- Oh, the synagogues? They were bombed by the British. I said, oh, really? Have you ever heard of Kristallnacht, when the synagogues were burnt by the Nazis? He changed color slightly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I found a hall. And we had a service in Hamburg. And the German, when he's in defeat, cringes. But he bounces back very quickly. When I came back to England on a short visit and had to go back to Germany, and we had to go by train from the Hook of Holland, a German railway official comes in and asks for our tickets. And there were some other British officers. And I said to them, got to show him our ticket? And he said, this is after the war. The German becomes stolz again, sort of very proud of his situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we noticed this, so much so that I remember long after I had left the army in the end of 1945, I came back to Germany to attend the international-- the, the consecration of the international memorial at Belsen, over which presided President Huess, the president of, of Germany. I arrived-- went via Hamburg, went by air. I arrived at the Hamburg airport. And we were in those days, only allowed to take out five pounds sterling from England. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I go through the, the necessary barriers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm asked by a German official, how much money have you got? I said, five pounds-- I said in German. Not much? And I said, too much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the attitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was met by a Jew who was a representative of the local Jewish community-- so-called, who I had heard, understood he had married a non-Jewish woman. And therefore, he had somewhat been saved from Germany. And he was also limping very badly. He'd been wounded in the First World War. So-- and then I noticed Hamburg was en fete. Shops were full of food. Lights were on. This was December, just before Christmas. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to this man, strange, you know. You know what's happening in England? Food is on ration, clothes are, are on ration. And here, look-- the shops are full. The lights are on. Ah, he said, lost the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, when a Jew says to me, lost the war, I had a little disturbance in the pit of my stomach. I thought to myself, what is this? What's happening? What sort of attitude is been having-- is developing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are what I call the 'little pinpricks' that one had to put up with as, as time passed. And I suppose, looking back now after 50 years, some memories fade. But some memories remain very vivid. The memories of the horror that you can't forget-- I can't recall the smell. I know it was there. But I've got vivid pictures in my mind of skeletons, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pictures of human suffering and of human courage, the courage to survive, the courage to feel that the Jewish people will survive in spite of everything. These are kind of things that are left me as I now sit back, after 50 years, and think what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said before about you wondered whether the reason why you had the bureaucratic difficulties was whether it was ignorance or prejudice or whatever, did you-- do you have an answer, your own answer to that question?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm still puzzled by it, even, even after sort of a half century's gone by. I'd like to be charitable and say, perhaps it was too big a problem. After all, we were all overtaken by events. We weren't prepared for this sort of thing. We weren't prepared militarily. We weren't prepared medically. We weren't prepared psychologically to be able to deal with a problem as vast as this. Now, if I, as a Jew, wasn't prepared as it were-- although I knew that, that these things have been happening. We'd, we'd got the news from 1942 onwards. We heard about Auschwitz and the destruction of the communities. But for these non-Jewish chaps, to them it was utterly new. So I can, in a way, sympathize with them because they didn't understand the situation. And it was too big for them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sometimes a situation which is-- goes out of hand, it's because people aren't equipped to deal with it. They weren't trained for this sort of thing. Military government wasn't trained for it. I give you a simple example. In the early days of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very concerned. We needed help. There was only two-- there were only two chaplains in the whole of Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I knew that the Jewish refugees-- the Jewish committee for relief abroad had a team in Holland. And we needed help. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I rushed out to Brussels to the headquarters of military government-- or civil affairs, they called it in those days. And I said, look, this team in, in, in Holland, really hasn't got enough work to do. And we need them desperately. And this young major-- same rank as myself, so I could talk to him quite openly. He said, well, you see, Padre, you see that map? He shows me a map with pins on it over the place. He says, these are all camps which exist in Europe. As far as we're concerned, it's an academic problem. I said, I beg your pardon? Academic? These people are dying like flies. And you sit here on your backside here in Brussels and tell me this is academic?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said something more than 'sitting on your backside.' But that's what I meant. I said, unless this team is brought over as quickly as possible, I'm going to report you for negligence to the general. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Belsen. There was a message waiting for me. The other commander, no, the, the senior staff officer of, of the Second Army had seen me in Belsen. And he realized I needed help. What do I require? How many chaplains can I-- can I get? So, off the top of my head, I said, five. And he cabled War Office to send over five chaplains. Well, the, the people he sent over weren't a particular quality that I would have liked. But at least I got a response. From-- not from this so-and-so sitting in Brussels on his backside.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, once you are there and you saw what was there, then you felt something had to be done. But in Brussels, he didn't know what was going on. He was sitting in cafes at night and enjoying himself. So this was the sort of attitude.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you see, it's a very mixed emotion. The whole thing is very confused. And even now, after all these years you still can't clarify it by saying that this was a reason for this, where this happened, so and so. It wasn't a sequence in that sense. So I imagine your-- although you knew what was going on, the actual reality of it was obviously very different experience to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody could visualize it. Unless you've seen it, you can't visualize it. I mean, we heard what was happening in Auschwitz. We heard people had been transported, you know, for-- to the east. And then we heard rumors of, of the gas chambers, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't visualize it. I mean, you go to a cremation today, you don't see it. You see the body being removed, very gently, very dignified, right? Doors open, the body gets carried away, doors close.

speaker: interviewee
text: But to, to think of people being thrown into a, a living-- a wide-open furnace, as it were, and being, being gassed by the-- being closed in a chamber and, and gassed by throwing this Zyklon gas through the-- through the-- some channel or other, you can't visualize it. You can't visualize it. I never saw a gas chamber, I must admit. But, but, but I can now visualize it, having seen what corpses are like, what hunger is like, and what disease is like. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can still see a picture in my mind of bodies being slung into, into this camp-- dreadful, dreadful thought, slung into mass graves of bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no-- the-- I'm used to burials. I'm used to officiating at a funeral, where the body is dignified. The coffin is put in, lowered in the ground, and so on. But to take a skeleton and chuck it in with no dignity, then where is the, the personality? Gone. The so-called human image disappears.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- this is a horrible picture that remains with you still. And when I see these pictures that are being reproduced-- as they are periodically, and they probably will be for God knows how many years to come-- there-- it all comes back very vividly. And I say to myself, you've seen it on the screen. It's nothing compared with reality. And when my dear friend, Lord Bernstein, who came over to Belsen shortly after liberation with an official team of cameramen-- and he took a film. And his idea was that he would show this film. He actually got local German officials to come and watch the bodies being buried, thrown into-- [SIGHS] the British authorities wouldn't allow that film to be shown. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was most upset. I knew Bernstein very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason given?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't give a reason. But they felt it was-- it would militate against good relations with Germans after the war. 40 years after the liberation, I had a brain wave. I organized a reunion of ministry personnel who had any connection with Belsen. And I wrote to the national press up and down the country-- and local press, hundreds and hundreds of newspapers-- saying that I was going to hold a reunion of anybody who had any military connection with Belsen. And several hundred men came down to London, spent the day with us. And Lord Bernstein put his film at my disposal. It was the first time it was shown. And he introduced it. He was then 80-odd years of age. And as he spoke about it, that poor man broke down into tears. It was a moving occasion. And he said, this is a film I wanted to show. And it's taken 40 years for it to be shown. Then later on it appeared on Radio 4. And well, that was the situation-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] certain reluctance to revive these memories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I think Spielberg's doing a good thing in keeping the archives going. Because those of us who have personal memories, we are getting older. We won't have much longer to live. And so here we are, telling the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- this is the first time, perhaps, or second time you've done a video. But you have written about it. Can you tell-- talk about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote this little book called which appeared some six months or so ago. Again, I was-- I was one of those people who felt reluctant to put it on, to write about it. I'd written about my experiences in the Middle East. Somehow I was reluctant to put it on print. And it was only when I was approached by young Rosensaft, who was born in Belsen, who came over to linger on a visit. And he said to me, you must, you must, you must. For no other reason, he said, do it for my father's sake. His father was dead by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sat down to write-- to gather all the information. I had some memoranda, which are going yellow with age, letters I'd written to my wife. I reread them. And it all came flooding back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I put this down on paper. And it was with me as long as I could write it, day and night, because it disturbed my night's sleep. And when I finished writing it, I had a heart attack, which is not surprising. But it's in print now. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know how many people will read it. But I hope they will. It'll-- it contains some of the memoranda which I wrote, letters-- some letters I wrote to my wife, who, poor girl, had to suffer all my frustrations. Because again, who could I write to? Who could I talk to, except my typewriter? And she was at the other end, the receiving end. So that's the situation. That's my story, as it were. So the memories were sort of somewhere held back in you until--

speaker: interviewee
text: The memories were there. But I had a tremendous reluctance to put it on paper. I thought-- I had a horrible feeling. I didn't know what it was. The idea of putting it in a book and then getting royalties for writing it somehow seemed all wrong. I haven't had any royalties now anyhow. But whether they would say-- the idea of-- I wouldn't-- anybody to say, he's writing, you know, to cash in on his memories. This was a horrible feeling. I mean, some people are reluctant to talk about it because it brings back sad memories or a sense of guilt, or whatever it is. But for me, it was quite different. I had no sense of guilt about this. But then-- that there was this terrible reluctance to put it down. I don't know. Something held me back, as I say, until the catalyst, when Menachem Rosensaft arrived. And when he talked about his father, then I realized he was right. His mother's still alive in New York. But Yossele died.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had been in Belsen too, his mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was a doctor. And she worked very closely with, with Brigadier Glyn Hughes. As a matter of fact, he had a great admiration for her. Because A, she had the knowledge, B, she had the language. She could talk to people. And she spoke Polish. She spoke German. She spoke French. She was-- she was an amazing woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a survivor herself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes, she was caught in it. And she and Yossele, they became very close. I think they were living together for a while. And then they married. And as a result, Menachem was born. He was a brilliant, young-- brilliant, young lawyer. And he did a wonderful job because he created the what they call, children of the survivors, first generation. I think they called them the 'Second Generation' because they were-- and did a wonderful job in America that-- they had a memorable conference they invited me to. And I spoke about my, my own experience. 1,700 young people came at their own expense from all over the country to New York to spend four days discussing it. And they had 42 workshops. It was unbelievable. That was the kind of attitude and the behavior of these young people. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to ask a few more things in my-- in my mind to ask you. Just going back a bit, as chaplain, senior chaplain to the British forces, you, presumably-- your first responsibility was to the forces rather than to--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're quite right. What I did was very simple. When it reached a crisis of, of seeing the actual situation in Belsen, I sent a circular around, certain letter, to all the Jewish personnel in the army, saying that the situation is such that I'm afraid my time must be given to them, not to you. And I'm sure I will get your sympathy. And you can do something, too. Let me have your cigarette and your chocolate rations, which they did. And I was inundated with their chocolates and cigarettes, which I could distribute to those who were capable of using it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nobody ever held that against me that I wasn't-- although the military authorities could very well have said, your primary job is fully-- to the army. But my Jewish conscience said, no. My responsibility is to these people. And men realized that. And while they would-- created difficulties, while the military government created difficulties on one side, it sounds like they did let you do that. They--

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't stop me. [LAUGHS] It's very simple. You see, I was-- the Jewish chaplain was in a very strange position. Nominally, he's responsible to his Christian superior, called the assistant chaplain general. But in fact, he's autonomous. Nobody can tell him where to go, what to do. Because he does his own job. And since he's-- I'm not attached to any particular unit, but I've got the whole area at my disposal. So if you want me there, I'm over there. Because they can't pin me down except when I am reporting back to headquarters to my own office. So it's, it's a very different situation. An ordinary army chaplain is attached to one unit or one regiment, really, or one brigade. But I wasn't. I had the whole army at my disposal. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you've said also about all the various things that were going on repatriation. How did it-- how did it sort of end? How did you-- your coming back and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the ending of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --I came back. It was the end of 1945. But that wasn't the end of Belsen. Belsen continued for some time. They had-- some wonderful people came. ORT came in and opened up workshops. The relief team did a wonderful job. They organized schools for, for the youngsters. Those children were being born in the meantime as well. And the, the Central Committee was operating. And this continued. I can't remember when Belsen actually was dissolved, when ultimately everybody disappeared. It was several years, anyhow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So as far as I'm concerned, I was only conscious of what happened whilst I was there. We had a number of visitors who came in. We had a-- they organized a congress, in which they invited some people from England, from the World Jewish Congress. We had Sydney Silverman, a member of parliament-- came over.

speaker: interviewee
text: A number of people, visitors, came over, you know. Everybody came, wanted to have a look and see what was happening. They didn't manage to, to achieve very much. But they could report back. It was a very good place visitors to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: You sound a little bit cynical about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am cynical. Because too many people used-- came and said, I was in Belsen. I visited Belsen. I saw what was going on. They didn't live there. They didn't go through the, the agonies of Belsen that we did. But anyway, that happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came back at the end of '45. Why, why did you come back at that point and not stay longer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my, my army service finished. You know, we work on the basis of, of you got a number. When your number's up, then you get your-- here's your release. And I was demobilized.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came back to London and ultimately settled down to civilian life, but became very much involved with the refugees-- still continued working with refugees, because you, you can't do that. You can never avoid. And I'm still involved with them to an extent. In what capacity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm available if they ever want me. I, I very much support the survivor center, which exists in London. Now I'm afraid that even the advancing years, it doesn't diminish one's, one's sense of responsibility to these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came back and became a community rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I came back to Hamstead Synagogue, which I was there for nearly 20 years. And then I did other things besides. But that was not-- that didn't have very much to do with the war. I became very much involved with Palestine-- Israel. Afterwards, I left the active ministry to become the director of the Jewish National Fund. I was very much involved with Israel, until I reached retirement-- so-called retirement age. That was 20 years ago. [LAUGHS] That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back over everything-- of the-- particularly, the time during the war, is there a message that you would like to pass on to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a kind of message, which everybody who was conscious of what happened there would say. Don't forget. You once-- once you forget, then you're asking for it all over again. And unfortunately, we are being reminded all too often. The so-called struggle for anti-Semitism-- against anti-Semitism is there still. We have it in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I think that we have in this country an organization called the-- the-- what do they call themselves? Charter 18. 18-- what's the 18 stand for? One, eight in the alphabet is AH, Adolf Hitler. It's a fascist organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the British National Movement and so on-- the fascism is there. All right, they're irritants at the moment. But they are-- little fleas can create a lot of irritation, a lot of disturbance. And they are. And unfortunately, the seeds of Hitler's doctrine have been well-sown. And it springs up all over the place. Germany's got it. France has got it. England's got it. America has got it. Klu Klux Klan still exists. So it's an ongoing struggle. We dare not-- we dare not forget. And that is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add before we finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you'd like to add before we-- before we finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I can think of-- I can think of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Levy, thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pleasure.
